frameworks.name: local-global deviance paradigm
frameworks.one_line: Dissociates stimulus-probability deviance from sequence-structure deviance

frameworks.name: neuromodulatory systems mapping
frameworks.one_line: Maps deviance responses across noradrenergic, dopaminergic, cholinergic, serotonergic nuclei

frameworks.name: locus coeruleus phasic arousal
frameworks.one_line: Tests whether LC selectively signals deviance or shares it with other neuromodulators

frameworks.name: pupillometry as central arousal marker
frameworks.one_line: Pupil size used as peripheral readout of central arousal

core_question: Do behaviorally-relevant deviant events selectively trigger locus coeruleus responses or also engage other subcortical neuromodulatory systems?
key_assumptions: Brainstem-optimized fMRI can resolve small neuromodulatory nuclei
Local-global paradigm separates probability-based from sequence-based deviance
manipulations: Task-relevant deviant detection across local-global conditions
key_findings: Deviant fMRI responses were distributed across LC and other subcortical nuclei and cortical areas
Both deviance types elicited pupil, LC, and other neuromodulatory system responses
Same multiple subcortical systems were recruited across computationally different deviance types
interpretations: Detection of task-relevant deviance recruits a shared multi-neuromodulatory subcortical network rather than LC alone
Computationally distinct deviance types converge onto overlapping neuromodulatory substrates
